drupalNodeEvent.title: Religion, Secularism, and Love as a Political Discourse in Modern China

field_speakers.processed:
Professor Guo Ting (Chinese University of Hong Kong)
Guo Ting studies religion, politics, and gender in transnational Asia. She is currently Assistant Professor of Cultural and Religious Studies, Chinese University of Hong Kong and incoming Assistant Professor of Language Studies, University of Toronto. She is the book reviewer editor for the Journal of the American Academy of Religion and a Diversity and Equity Committee representative for the Association for Asian Studies. She co-hosts a podcast called 時差 in-betweenness (@shichapodcast).

field_organizers.processed: Hong Kong Institute for the Humanities and Social Sciences, The University of Hong Kong

drupalNodeEvent.field_event_venue: Rm 201, May Hall, The University of Hong Kong

field_description.processed: What is the meaning of love in modern Chinese politics? Why has ai (love) been a crucial political discourse for secular nationalism for generations of political leaders as a powerful instrument to the present day? This book offers the first systematic examination of the ways in which the notion of love has been introduced, adapted, and engineered as a political discourse for the building and rebuilding of a secular modern nation, all the while appropriating Confucianism, Christianity, popular religion, ghost stories, political religion, and their religious affects. The insights of this exploration expand not only the discussion of the role of emotions in the project of Chinese modernity, but also the study of affective governance and religious nationalisms around the world today.
